skill.name: systematic-debugging
skill.description: Guide AI coding agents through a four-phase debugging process that finds root causes before proposing fixes.
skill.summary: Apply root cause analysis before fixing bugs, flaky tests, and unexpected behavior.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown examples, Graphviz labels, and TypeScript template strings. Confirmed risks are limited to legitimate debugging guidance that may inspect environment variables, macOS signing identities, files, and local test commands. No evidence found of malicious intent, network exfiltration, persistence, or prompt injection.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Diagnostic Command Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends diagnostic examples that list macOS keychains, signing identities, and environment-derived signing values. This is legitimate debugging guidance, but it can expose sensitive operational metadata in logs if used without redaction.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and environment reads are directly present and semantically tied to debugging signing and git-init failures. There is no network sink or credential theft, so the risk is exposure through local logs rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Executable Local Test Bisection Script
medium_findings.description: The included shell script accepts a path and test pattern, enumerates matching files, runs npm tests, and displays file metadata. The behavior is transparent and local, but marketplace users should know it executes project tests and inspects filesystem paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script is executable tooling with clear local side effects, including running npm test. Arguments are quoted and there is no network access or persistence, which lowers confidence that it is dangerous.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Mostly From Documentation
low_findings.description: Many external command detections are Markdown code fences, Graphviz diagrams, or TypeScript template strings used to explain debugging concepts. These are not hidden execution paths, but they may cause agents to suggest running local commands during debugging.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation diagrams or error-message template strings, not runtime shell execution. The surrounding files are instructional material for debugging.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography, but the reviewed locations contain timeout examples, dates, prose, or Graphviz labels. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, or other weak cryptographic implementation in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the flagged contexts found no cryptographic API usage. The detections appear to come from short tokens or line content unrelated to cryptography.

low_findings.title: Pressure Test Files Use Coercive Scenario Language
low_findings.description: The pressure-test files ask an agent to make decisions under urgency, authority, and exhaustion. This is test content for the skill, not an instruction to bypass marketplace review, and no prompt-injection override phrases were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The language is directive and could influence an agent if loaded out of context. It is clearly framed as validation pressure tests and does not claim system authority or ask to skip security analysis.

dangerous_patterns.title: macOS Keychain and Signing Identity Enumeration
dangerous_patterns.description: Diagnostic examples include listing keychains and signing identities. Users should redact output before sharing logs and avoid running these commands unless signing diagnostics are required.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact macOS security and codesign commands are present. The context is defensive debugging, not exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Variable Inspection in Debug Logs
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples inspect NODE_ENV and signing-related environment values. This can be safe when limited, but users should avoid logging secrets or complete environment dumps.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Environment inspection is explicit in the examples. The reviewed content narrows the examples to debugging context and does not include a network destination.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Shell Script Executes Project Tests
dangerous_patterns.description: The bisection helper runs npm test for discovered files and checks for created filesystem state. This is expected behavior, but it can execute arbitrary project test code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script directly invokes npm test on files found from the provided pattern. There is no evidence of hidden payloads, network use, or persistence.

content.user_title: Debug bugs with root cause analysis
content.value_statement: Random fixes waste time and often create new failures. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured process for investigating bugs before changing code.
actual_capabilities: Guides a four-phase workflow: investigation, pattern analysis, hypothesis testing, and implementation.
Requires root cause evidence before proposing code fixes.
Shows how to trace bad values backward through call stacks and component boundaries.
Provides condition-based waiting patterns for flaky asynchronous tests.
Includes a local test bisection script for finding tests that create unwanted state.
Encourages defense-in-depth validation after the root cause is known.
limitations: It is a methodology skill, not an automated debugger.
Some examples involve local shell commands that users should review before running.
It does not provide language-specific debugger integrations.
It may take longer than a quick patch when the root cause is unclear.

use_cases.title: Investigate production bugs
use_cases.description: Use the phases to gather evidence, trace failures, and avoid risky emergency patches.

use_cases.title: Stabilize flaky tests
use_cases.description: Replace arbitrary sleeps with condition-based waiting and isolate tests that create shared state.

use_cases.title: Review failed fixes
use_cases.description: Reset a stalled debugging session by documenting hypotheses, evidence, and architectural assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Start with evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic-debugging for this issue. First summarize the error, reproduction steps, and recent changes. Do not suggest a fix until the root cause is supported by evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Trace a failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply root cause tracing to this failure. Work backward from the symptom through each caller or data boundary. Identify where the bad value first appears.

prompt_templates.title: Fix a flaky test
prompt_templates.prompt: Use condition-based waiting to investigate this flaky test. Identify any arbitrary timeout, define the real condition to wait for, and propose a minimal verified change.

prompt_templates.title: Challenge the architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: We have tried three fixes and the issue remains. Use systematic-debugging to reassess the architecture, list failed hypotheses, and propose the next evidence-gathering step.

output_examples.input: A payment API fails intermittently during high traffic.
output: Phase 1: Confirm the exact error and reproduction pattern.
Phase 2: Compare failing requests with working services and recent deployments.
Phase 3: Test one hypothesis with targeted metrics for connection pool exhaustion.
Phase 4: Add the smallest fix and verify the failure no longer reproduces.

output_examples.input: A test sometimes passes only after adding longer sleeps.
output: Identify the real asynchronous condition behind the wait.
Replace the fixed delay with polling that has a clear timeout.
Run the test repeatedly and in parallel to verify the race is gone.

output_examples.input: A generated file appears after the test suite runs.
output: Start from the unexpected file and trace when it appears.
Use bisection to isolate the test that creates the state.
Fix the source of the bad path and add validation at each layer.

best_practices: Complete root cause investigation before proposing any fix.
Test one hypothesis at a time so results stay interpretable.
Redact environment values, keychain output, and filesystem paths before sharing diagnostic logs.
anti_patterns: Adding a quick patch before understanding why the bug happens.
Combining multiple fixes in one attempt and losing the ability to isolate cause.
Logging full environments or signing details into shared channels.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides debugging guidance and examples. Users should review any suggested command before running it.

faq.question: Is it useful for simple bugs?
faq.answer: Yes. The process can be brief, but it still asks for evidence before a fix.

faq.question: Can it help with flaky tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes condition-based waiting guidance and a local bisection helper for test pollution.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes local shell tooling and examples that inspect environment and signing state. These are legitimate but need user care.

faq.question: Does it require a specific programming language?
faq.answer: No. The workflow is language-independent, although examples use TypeScript, Bash, and command-line tools.

faq.question: How should sensitive diagnostics be handled?
faq.answer: Run only relevant diagnostics, avoid full environment dumps, and redact secrets or signing identifiers before sharing output.
